text.content:
Rel Astra is older than Hardby and the conspiracy operates here from a position of established authority rather than reactive defense. The Pressure Clock for Phase 4 is shorter (four segments) but the segments hit harder. The party should expect to reach segment 2 or 3 by the end of the phase regardless of what they do; the question is whether segment 4 fires.

The clock at a glance — four escalating rhythms

  1. Polite obstruction — the harbor commission's procedural calendar slows. Records take days instead of hours. Audiences are rescheduled. Halask's filings are docketed in the wrong queue.
  2. Witness pressure — Halask's old Aerdi colleagues become reluctant to discuss the case. A junior partner the party was cultivating receives a transfer offer to Rauxes. Mirelth or Vesh receives a private warning and becomes harder to reach.
  3. Witness loss — one of the party's contacts in Rel Astra is taken or killed. Astor's chandlery is broken into and partially burned (he survives, his records do not). Or the imperial advocate handling Lyla's case is "reassigned" before Halask can resolve it. Or, in extremis, Halask himself is found dead at the Brass Sextant — Path 1 closes.
  4. The seating proceeds — the harbor commission convenes the candidate vote on the schedule the conspiracy planned, before the party can prevent it. Mirelth is held over (Ruth's procedural objection); Vesh and Tenrel are seated 3-1. The campaign's central stakes have been lost; Phase 4's resolution becomes a question of damage control rather than victory.

Triggers — what advances the clock

TriggerSegments
Arrival under Official Delegation+2 immediate (the conspiracy has had three weeks to prepare)
Arrival under Commercial Cover+1 (the conspiracy assumes the party is in the city, prepares for it)
Arrival under Quiet Pursuit+0
The party files an indictment with the Aerdi Commercial Court (Path 1, day 5)+1
The party publicly disqualifies Mirelth (Three Seats > Mirelth, option 2)+1
The party briefs Galenix at the Naelax embassy+1 (the embassy leaks the visit within a day)
The party visits Sereth's offices openly more than twice without an audience scheduled+1
The party kills any senior C&V Rel Astra staff member (Path 3, or any escalation)+2 immediate
Each full session day in which the party makes no investigative progress in Rel Astra+1

Milestones — what happens at each segment

Segment 1 — Polite obstruction

The conspiracy's reaction is procedural and deniable. Records the party requests are "in transit" for three days and arrive incomplete. Audiences are politely rescheduled. Halask's first filing is mis-docketed and requires re-submission. The harbor commission's clerk is suddenly very busy. None of this prevents the party from acting; all of it costs them time. Time advances the clock further.

Segment 2 — Witness pressure

The conspiracy reaches into the party's social and procedural network. Mirelth becomes harder to reach: he leaves Rel Astra for his country estate "for personal reasons" and the disqualification documents must now be served on him by Aerdi rural courier (an additional ten days). Vesh becomes more anxious: his sister-in-law's bond is doubled and the case is moved up on the imperial advocate's calendar. One of Halask's old colleagues declines to provide an expected affidavit. The party can recover from any of these individually; recovering from all of them at once costs Halask one full week of work.

Segment 3 — Witness loss

The conspiracy reaches into the party directly. GM picks one based on what would hurt most:

  • Astor — his chandlery is broken into and partially burned. He survives but his records (signed statements, contact information for the four uncompromised council members) do not. He withdraws from active cooperation. The Old Harbor Ward Council route closes for the rest of the phase.
  • The imperial advocate handling Lyla Vesh's case is "reassigned" to a Rauxes posting before Halask can complete the procedural challenge. Vesh's withdrawal becomes contingent on the new advocate, who is harder to predict. Vesh stays on the candidate slate until the matter is resolved; the seating risks proceeding regardless.
  • Sayan (Halask's old colleague) is found drowned in the harbor. The deposition Halask was preparing through her is lost. Halask is shaken. The procedural path slows by another week.
  • Halask himself is found dead at the Brass Sextant. This is the worst single outcome. Path 1 closes entirely. Path 2 becomes the only remaining political option. The party's procedural-court framework dies with Halask. Use this only if the party has been visibly reckless and earned the consequence.

Segment 4 — The seating proceeds

The harbor commission convenes the candidate vote on the schedule the conspiracy planned. Vesh and Tenrel are seated 3-1. Mirelth is held over (Ruth's procedural objection works as planned, but for the conspiracy's benefit, not the party's). The conspiracy's harbor-commission play succeeds. Phase 4's resolution becomes a question of damage control rather than victory.

The party can still indict Sereth after this happens — but the seating cannot be undone for at least two years (the next council confirmation cycle), and the conspiracy now controls the swing votes on every discretionary harbor matter for that period.

If segment 4 fires, the campaign continues into Phase 5 with the conspiracy operationally embedded and the party reduced to documenting its consequences.

How to use the clock at the table

  • Show the players the clock face. Four segments, visible.
  • When you advance, say which trigger fired and what the consequence is.
  • Phase 4's clock is faster than Phase 3's because Rel Astra is denser and the conspiracy is at home. Don't soften the witness loss in segment 3 — that segment is supposed to hurt.
  • The clock should reach 2–3 segments by the end of Phase 4 in a normal-paced campaign. Segment 4 should fire only if the party has been reckless or has refused both procedural and political paths and tried only kinetic ones.

Phase 4 does not name "E." Phase 4 does not even confirm the writer is non-human. It only adds a fourth and a fifth observation to Veska's three from Phase 3, and lets the party assemble a clearer portrait of a hand that does not belong to anyone in the Aerdy realm.

Where the new instances appear

Instance 4 — at the Aerdi imperial registry

If the party investigates the harbor commission's vacancy paperwork through the imperial registry (Halask can arrange access; the registrar is one of his old commercial-court contacts), they will find that the leave-of-absence vacancy (the third one, the one Tenrel is candidate for) was created by a single ceremonial document filed by the harbor commission's senior clerk eighteen months ago. The document itself is unremarkable. The marginal annotation, in a hand the registrar will note he has never seen before, reads: "Sufficient. Hold open until the second autumn after the next." The hand matches Veska's three observations and adds two more:

  • Fourth observation: the writer holds the quill in a way that produces uniformly thick downstrokes — implying either a blunted nib used deliberately, or a hand that does not pivot the quill the way Aerdy and Yeomanry hands do. Halask, asked, will recall that some Suel hands write this way, but only certain very old families.
  • Fifth observation: the wax used to seal the original cover of the document is not Aerdi imperial wax (which is a specific recipe of beeswax and pine resin tinted with cinnabar). It is a darker, harder wax that left a slight black residue on the paper around the seal. Halask cannot identify it. He has, in his career, seen exactly one similar wax — on a single document filed in Aerdi commercial court in 1571 by an envoy who claimed to represent "a southern trading concern" that no one ever found again.

Instance 5 — at Sereth's offices (Path 2 only)

If the party negotiates Path 2 with Sereth and asks him directly about the "E." annotations, his response (quoted in his NPC entry under What Sereth will not concede > The annotation hand) itself constitutes the fifth-and-final observation: that the writer exists, is foreign by some significant margin, has been advising the firm for at least the better part of a decade, and is not a person Sereth has ever met or expects to meet. If the party also accessed instance 4 at the registry, they have a portrait of an annotator who is not Aerdi, not Yeomanry, possibly Suel by hand-form, possibly using non-Aerdi sealing wax, and is at the operational level of the conspiracy itself. They have not named her. They have, however, learned to look for her.

What the party should NOT learn in Phase 4

  • The annotator's name.
  • The annotator's species.
  • The annotator's location.
  • The annotator's organization (House Eilserv, the Vault, Erelhei-Cinlu, Drow politics, the Demon Queen, etc. — none of these surface).
  • That there is more than one annotator. (There is only one, in this conspiracy. Other operators may be visible later.)

What the party SHOULD learn in Phase 4

  • That the conspiracy has a head, and the head is not Sereth, and the head is not in the Aerdy realm.
  • That the head writes — physically writes, with a quill, on paper — and that the writing has identifiable features that can be tracked.
  • That the conspiracy's plans are at least decade-scale, drawn down to the level of which seats become vacant in which months in which years.
  • That the campaign's larger picture is much older than Loftwick, much older than Hardby, and probably much older than the Aerdy realm itself.

How to play this thread at the table

Drip-feed. The fourth observation should arrive in mid-phase, in the registry research scene; the fifth should arrive late, either in Sereth's office or — if Path 2 was not pursued — in a third small registry detail Halask uncovers in the campaign's last two days. Players should leave Phase 4 with a question, not an answer.

GM-only note: The annotator is Eclavdra of House Eilserv, of the Drow city of Erelhei-Cinlu in the deep underdark, currently in the early stages of the long political move that will, decades hence, become the campaign described in Vault of the Drow. The harbor-revenue play in Rel Astra is, for her, a single small piece of a much larger long-term operation: positioning surface-world commercial allies whose continued operation will provide cover and resources for House Eilserv's much slower and much larger move. Phase 5, if the campaign continues, may eventually take the party to the Hellfurnaces, the Sulm desert, or to the older underdark. Phase 4 does not.

Read aloud at the moment the party leaves Rel Astra. The text below is route-and-resolution-agnostic. Adjust the small details based on the path the party took (indictment filed and trial pending; Sereth resigned; Sereth dead in his office; etc.).

Rel Astra in your last hour does not change. The harbor's clock chimes the seventh bell of morning and every other clock in the city chimes with it, the way Hardby's never quite did. The Aerdi sun is high already. The breeze off the Sea of Gearnat carries the same lemon-and-dust-and-clean-salt smell that the city carried on your first morning, and will carry on every morning until the seas swallow it.

You are leaving. You are leaving with what you came to take, or what you could take, or what was left to be taken after the part you wanted had already been moved.

You carry, in a sealed dispatch case under Halask's procedural seal, a copy of the Aerdi Commercial Court filing that will, in ninety days, return Tarlith Vhal Sereth a verdict — or you carry a folded private statement that Sereth signed in his own working office and gave you, in exchange for being allowed to retire to a country estate that has been waiting for him for ten years — or you carry the lead of a hand-written list of the items you were able to recover from the strongroom in the four minutes you had before the Aerdi watch arrived. The shape is different in each case. The weight is the same.

You have left, behind you in the city, the names you have learned. Therion Halask, who will write to Caelith every two weeks for as long as either of them is in office, and who will, in his small careful way, continue to file the procedural motions that keep the conspiracy from finding the easy path it wants. Belven Astor, who will continue to lose 4-3 votes on the Old Harbor Ward Council and continue to bring them. Lord Galenix Naelax, who is now either your ally or your problem, depending on what you chose. The eight others, smaller and more various, whose names you may or may not have learned by the time you left.

You also carry a question. The annotation hand. Five observations, none of them sufficient, all of them recurring. A wax that is not Aerdi imperial wax. A hand that is not Aerdi or Yeomanry or any Suel hand any of you have read. A planning horizon that begins ten years before any of you were attached to this work. Sereth — if you let him speak it — said: "Not from this side of the great desert. I do not know whether the writer is a person, an office, or a guild." Halask, asked the same question, says: "If I had to guess, I would not."

The Lacquer Wave is at the merchant quay, or your carriage is at the Customs Quay, or your horses are at the West Gate stables, or you are leaving on foot through the South Gate. It does not matter which. You are going home.

Caelith's Letter Six is in your pack. You will not read it again on the road. You read it once, twice, three times in your last evening in Rel Astra, and you have remembered the only line you needed to remember: "The conspiracy you have named is not the conspiracy."

The harbor falls behind. The pale walls fall behind. The Old Harbor Ward and the Goldsmiths' Quarter and the Naelax embassy and Sereth's offices and Halask's two rooms above the pastry shop and the Brass Sextant on the third quay — all of it falls behind.

You ride west.

What Phase 4 leaves the party with

  • Sereth's outcome: indicted (Path 1), resigned and exiled (Path 2), or killed (Path 3).
  • The candidate slate: Mirelth withdrawn or disqualified; Vesh withdrawn (if Halask's resolution of Lyla's case completed) or seated (if it did not); Tenrel seated or unseated (depending on whether the indictment or the imperial-seat brief succeeded).
  • The harbor-commission play: stopped for at least two years (Paths 1 or 2 with full intervention); paused for six to twelve months (Path 3); succeeded (segment-4 outcome — campaign continues with the conspiracy operationally embedded).
  • The Aerdy claimant: Galenix briefed and an ally; Galenix unbriefed and an unwitting tool; or Galenix used and now a debtor of the party.
  • The annotation hand: five observations on file. The writer remains unnamed. The writer has been confirmed to exist and to operate at the conspiracy's planning level.
  • The institutional aftermath: Caelith's standing partially restored or further damaged depending on path; Halask continuing to be a Rel Astra correspondent; Astor continuing to be the party's Rel Astra civic ally.

Where the campaign goes from here

This concludes Blooming Rot, Part 2. The campaign's larger arc — the one that points at the underdark and the writer of the marginalia — is left open for a possible Part 3. The questions Part 3 might address:

  • Who is the writer of the "E." annotations? (Spoiler: Eclavdra of House Eilserv, but the campaign should never name her until and unless the party reaches the underdark.)
  • What is the older shape behind the harbor-revenue play? (The Suel-Drow long-game; the Vault of the Drow as positional asset; the slow-burning Demon Queen worship; House Eilserv's century-scale operations.)
  • Where does Haskur Vandrell end up — in Greyhawk City, in the Hellfurnaces, in the Vault itself? (Vandrell is a small piece. He may be reused as a Part 3 antagonist, or he may simply be the operative who survived this round and was reassigned; either is fine.)
  • What does Caelith do next? (Probably retire, possibly within two years; the Audit Hall's structural problems will outlive his time in office.)
  • What does Trina do next? (This is Trina's question and the GM's; it depends on the player who runs Alicia and on the campaign's appetite for fey-adjacent threads.)

If Part 3 happens, it begins with Caelith's letter inviting the party back to Loftwick to discuss "a small matter that may not be small." It does not begin in the underdark. It begins in a civic room in Loftwick that the party already knows.
